sections.title: 🧒 The Story
sections.rawMarkdown:
```
Every team says "the system is down."
But until you can answer these three questions, you are still guessing:
→ what is broken?
→ where is it broken?
→ since when?

Observability is what turns panic into diagnosis.

METRICS answer:
→ is the system healthy right now?

LOGS answer:
→ what exactly happened?

TRACES answer:
→ where did the time go across services?

AUDIT TRAILS answer:
→ who changed what?

MENTAL MODEL:
Monitoring tells you there is pain.
Observability helps you explain the pain quickly enough to fix it.
```

---

→ where did the time go across services?

sections.title: 📊 CloudWatch — Deep Dive
sections.rawMarkdown:
### Metrics
```
NAMESPACE: logical grouping of metrics
→ AWS/EC2, AWS/Lambda, AWS/RDS, YourApp/BusinessMetrics

DIMENSIONS: attributes to filter metrics
→ EC2: InstanceId=i-abc123, InstanceType=t3.medium
→ Lambda: FunctionName=myFunction, Resource=myFunction:PROD

RESOLUTION:
Standard: 1-minute granularity (default for most AWS services)
High-resolution: 1-second granularity (for your custom metrics)

BUILT-IN METRICS BY SERVICE:

EC2:
CPUUtilization, NetworkIn, NetworkOut, DiskReadOps, DiskWriteOps
StatusCheckFailed (hardware or software check failed)
NOT included by default: Memory, disk space (need CloudWatch Agent)

Lambda:
Invocations, Errors, Duration, Throttles, ConcurrentExecutions
DeadLetterErrors, IteratorAge (for stream-based triggers — staleness)

RDS/Aurora:
CPUUtilization, DatabaseConnections, FreeStorageSpace
ReadLatency, WriteLatency, FreeableMemory, ReplicaLag

ALB:
RequestCount, TargetResponseTime (latency), HTTPCode_Target_5XX_Count
HealthyHostCount, UnHealthyHostCount

SQS:
ApproximateNumberOfMessagesVisible (queue depth)
ApproximateAgeOfOldestMessage, NumberOfMessagesSent

CUSTOM METRICS:
aws cloudwatch put-metric-data \
  --namespace "MyApp/Business" \
  --metric-name "OrdersPlaced" \
  --value 47 \
  --unit Count \
  --dimensions Environment=Production

→ Track: active users, orders placed, revenue, API errors, custom business KPIs
→ Cost: $0.30/metric/month (first 10,000 metrics free)
```

### CloudWatch Alarms
```
ALARM STATES:
OK: metric within threshold
ALARM: metric breached threshold
INSUFFICIENT_DATA: not enough data to determine

ACTIONS ON ALARM:
→ SNS notification → email/Slack/PagerDuty
→ EC2 Auto Scaling: scale in or scale out
→ EC2 action: stop, terminate, reboot, recover instance
→ Systems Manager OpsItem

ALARM TYPES:
Metric Alarm: watch one metric vs threshold
Composite Alarm: combine multiple alarms with AND/OR logic
"ALARM if CPU > 80% AND Memory > 90% (both must be true)"

MUST-HAVE ALARMS FOR PRODUCTION:
EC2:      CPUUtilization > 80%, StatusCheckFailed > 0
Lambda:   Errors > 0.1%, Throttles > 0, Duration P99 > 5000ms
RDS:      CPUUtilization > 80%, DatabaseConnections > 80% of max
          FreeStorageSpace < 20% of total, ReplicaLag > 60s
ALB:      HTTPCode_Target_5XX_Count > 10, TargetResponseTime P99 > 2000ms
SQS:      ApproximateNumberOfMessagesVisible on DLQ > 0 (something failing!)
Billing:  EstimatedCharges > your monthly budget threshold
```

### CloudWatch Logs
```
LOG GROUP: container for a service's logs (/aws/lambda/myFunction)
LOG STREAM: one log stream per instance/invocation
RETENTION: 1 day to NEVER (default: never = costly! Set retention!)

SENDING LOGS:
→ Lambda: automatic (via execution role + awslogs)
→ EC2: install CloudWatch Agent (needed for app logs, not just console output)
→ ECS: awslogs log driver in task definition (no agent needed)
→ API Gateway: enable in stage settings
→ Your app: CloudWatch SDK or structured logging to stdout

CLOUDWATCH LOGS INSIGHTS (query language):
# Find all errors in last hour
fields @timestamp, @message
| filter @message like /ERROR/
| sort @timestamp desc
| limit 100

# Lambda cold start analysis
filter @type = "REPORT"
| stats avg(@initDuration), max(@initDuration) by bin(5m)

# P99 Lambda duration
filter @type = "REPORT"
| stats pct(@duration, 99) as p99Duration by bin(1h)

# Count 5xx errors per minute
filter status >= 500
| stats count(*) as error_count by bin(1m)
| sort error_count desc

METRIC FILTERS:
→ Extract metrics FROM log patterns
→ Example: count lines matching "ERROR" → publish as metric → alarm on it
→ Count specific exception type → metric → alarm
→ No need to instrument code separately if logs already contain the data

SUBSCRIPTION FILTERS:
→ Stream logs in real-time to Lambda (process) or Kinesis (stream)
→ Use for: real-time log analytics, sending to Elasticsearch, alerting
```

---

sections.title: 🔍 CloudTrail — Audit Trail
sections.rawMarkdown:
```
CloudTrail records EVERY API call in your AWS account.
Who did what, when, from where, with what result.

EVENT TYPES:
Management Events (control plane):
→ CreateBucket, DeleteInstance, CreateUser, AttachPolicy
→ Free for first copy in CloudTrail console (Event History: 90 days)
→ Additional trails: charges apply

Data Events (data plane):
→ S3 GetObject, PutObject, DeleteObject (object-level)
→ Lambda InvokeFunction
→ DynamoDB GetItem, PutItem
→ Not captured by default (very high volume, extra cost)
→ Enable selectively for sensitive buckets/functions

CLOUDTRAIL LAKE:
→ Managed data lake for CloudTrail events
→ Query with SQL via Athena integration
→ Run complex audit queries: "all API calls by user X in last 6 months"
→ Retention: 7 years (for compliance)

WHAT CLOUDTRAIL CAPTURES:
{
  "userIdentity": {
    "type": "IAMUser",
    "arn": "arn:aws:iam::123456:user/alice",
    "accessKeyId": "AKIA..."
  },
  "eventTime": "2024-01-15T14:30:00Z",
  "eventSource": "s3.amazonaws.com",
  "eventName": "DeleteObject",
  "awsRegion": "us-east-1",
  "sourceIPAddress": "203.0.113.50",
  "requestParameters": {
    "bucketName": "prod-customer-data",
    "key": "users/12345/personal.pdf"
  }
}

ENABLE CLOUDTRAIL BEST PRACTICES:
✅ Enable in ALL regions (attackers often use less-watched regions)
✅ Enable log file validation (detects tampering with logs)
✅ Store in dedicated S3 bucket (separate account ideally)
✅ Enable CloudWatch Logs integration (query + alarm on specific events)
✅ Retain logs: 1+ year for security, 7 years for compliance

USEFUL CLOUDTRAIL ALERTS:
→ Root account login (should never happen)
→ IAM policy change (unexpected permission escalation)
→ CloudTrail disabled (attacker covering tracks)
→ Failed console login 5+ times (brute force attempt)
→ New IAM user with admin access created
→ Security Group modified to allow port 22 from 0.0.0.0/0
```

---

blocks.content:
CloudTrail records EVERY API call in your AWS account.
Who did what, when, from where, with what result.

EVENT TYPES:

Data Events (data plane):

CLOUDTRAIL LAKE:

→ Query with SQL via Athena integration
→ Run complex audit queries: "all API calls by user X in last 6 months"
→ Retention: 7 years (for compliance)

WHAT CLOUDTRAIL CAPTURES:

  "userIdentity": {
    "type": "IAMUser",
    "arn": "arn:aws:iam::123456:user/alice",
    "accessKeyId": "AKIA..."
  },
  "eventTime": "2024-01-15T14:30:00Z",
  "eventSource": "s3.amazonaws.com",
  "eventName": "DeleteObject",
  "awsRegion": "us-east-1",
  "sourceIPAddress": "203.0.113.50",
  "requestParameters": {
    "bucketName": "prod-customer-data",
    "key": "users/12345/personal.pdf"
  }
}

ENABLE CLOUDTRAIL BEST PRACTICES:
✅ Enable in ALL regions (attackers often use less-watched regions)
✅ Enable log file validation (detects tampering with logs)
✅ Store in dedicated S3 bucket (separate account ideally)
✅ Enable CloudWatch Logs integration (query + alarm on specific events)
✅ Retain logs: 1+ year for security, 7 years for compliance

USEFUL CLOUDTRAIL ALERTS:
→ Root account login (should never happen)
→ IAM policy change (unexpected permission escalation)
→ CloudTrail disabled (attacker covering tracks)
→ Failed console login 5+ times (brute force attempt)

→ Security Group modified to allow port 22 from 0.0.0.0/0

sections.title: 🔭 X-Ray — Distributed Tracing
sections.rawMarkdown:
```
WHAT X-RAY DOES:
→ Traces requests through your entire application
→ Shows: which service handled it, how long each step took, where errors occurred
→ Works across: Lambda, EC2, ECS, API Gateway, SQS, DynamoDB, RDS, HTTP calls

WITHOUT X-RAY:
User reports: "my checkout is slow"
You: look at Lambda logs → look at RDS logs → look at external API logs...
→ Manually correlate across 5 different log streams
→ Takes hours to find the bottleneck

WITH X-RAY:
→ Single trace shows complete request journey
→ Timeline visualization: API Gateway (10ms) → Lambda (50ms) → RDS query (2000ms!)
→ Found it: RDS query is the bottleneck → add index → fixed

CONCEPTS:
Trace: one end-to-end request (has unique trace ID)
Segment: one service's contribution (Lambda segment, RDS segment)
Subsegment: detailed breakdown within a segment (specific DB query)
Annotation: key-value pairs for filtering traces (userId, orderId)
Metadata: additional data (not indexed, not searchable)

SERVICE MAP:
→ Visual graph of your architecture
→ Shows: services, their connections, error rates, latency
→ Color-coded health: green = healthy, yellow = slow, red = errors
→ Click a service → see its traces

ENABLE X-RAY:
Lambda: Enable Active Tracing in function config
         (or POWERTOOLS_TRACER_CAPTURE_RESPONSE=true env var)
ECS: Add X-Ray sidecar container to task definition
EC2: Install X-Ray daemon on instance
API Gateway: Enable X-Ray tracing on stage
SDK: Wrap SDK clients with X-Ray (automatic segment creation)

SAMPLING:
→ Tracing every request = high cost (large apps = millions of requests)
→ Sampling: trace a percentage of requests
→ Default: 1 request/second + 5% of additional requests
→ Custom rules: trace 100% of requests with errors, 1% of successful
→ Cost: $5.00 per million traces recorded (first 100K/month free)
```

---

blocks.content:
WHAT X-RAY DOES:
→ Traces requests through your entire application
→ Shows: which service handled it, how long each step took, where errors occurred
→ Works across: Lambda, EC2, ECS, API Gateway, SQS, DynamoDB, RDS, HTTP calls

CONCEPTS:

Subsegment: detailed breakdown within a segment (specific DB query)
Annotation: key-value pairs for filtering traces (userId, orderId)
Metadata: additional data (not indexed, not searchable)

SERVICE MAP:
→ Visual graph of your architecture
→ Shows: services, their connections, error rates, latency
→ Color-coded health: green = healthy, yellow = slow, red = errors
→ Click a service → see its traces

ENABLE X-RAY:
Lambda: Enable Active Tracing in function config

ECS: Add X-Ray sidecar container to task definition
EC2: Install X-Ray daemon on instance
API Gateway: Enable X-Ray tracing on stage
SDK: Wrap SDK clients with X-Ray (automatic segment creation)

SAMPLING:
→ Tracing every request = high cost (large apps = millions of requests)
→ Sampling: trace a percentage of requests
→ Default: 1 request/second + 5% of additional requests
→ Custom rules: trace 100% of requests with errors, 1% of successful
→ Cost: $5.00 per million traces recorded (first 100K/month free)

revisionPoints: → Traces requests through your entire application
→ Shows: which service handled it, how long each step took, where errors occurred
→ Works across: Lambda, EC2, ECS, API Gateway, SQS, DynamoDB, RDS, HTTP calls
→ Manually correlate across 5 different log streams
→ Takes hours to find the bottleneck
→ Single trace shows complete request journey
→ Timeline visualization: API Gateway (10ms) → Lambda (50ms) → RDS query (2000ms!)
→ Found it: RDS query is the bottleneck → add index → fixed

sections.title: 🎯 Interview Q&A — Observability
sections.rawMarkdown:
### Observability Real-World Failure Flow

```
User reports "system is down"
          │
          ▼
 Metrics show symptom
 (latency, errors, saturation)
          │
          ▼
 Logs give request / error context
          │
          ▼
 Traces show dependency path
          │
          ▼
 CloudTrail shows whether a change triggered it
          │
          ▼
 Root cause isolated

SENIOR MENTAL MODEL:
Metrics tell you there is a fire.
Logs and traces tell you where it is burning.
Audit trails tell you whether someone started it.
Good observability reduces investigation branching during incidents.
```

**Q: A service is 'slow', but CPU looks fine everywhere. How do you lead that investigation?**
```
"CPU being normal just tells me the bottleneck is elsewhere.

I would build a latency breakdown:
→ ALB/API Gateway latency
→ app latency
→ downstream database latency
→ cache hit rate
→ external API latency

Then I would use:
→ CloudWatch metrics for high-level symptoms
→ logs for error context and request IDs
→ X-Ray or tracing for end-to-end timeline

The key senior move is correlating one user request across systems instead of looking at
one dashboard at a time. Most 'slow app' incidents turn out to be a dependency,
lock contention, bad query plan, or retry storm rather than raw CPU shortage."
```

**Q: What logs, metrics, and alarms are mandatory before you call a system production-ready?**
```
"At minimum I want three layers:

Golden signals:
→ latency
→ traffic
→ errors
→ saturation

Business signals:
→ orders placed
→ checkout success rate
→ payment failures
→ queue age / backlog growth

Operational alarms:
→ error budget burn or 5xx spike
→ DLQ > 0
→ database connections near max
→ latency p95/p99 above threshold
→ no traffic when there should be traffic

And I want structured logs with correlation IDs.
Senior teams do not just log errors; they make incidents searchable and explainable."
```

**Q: CloudTrail shows who changed something, but how do you turn that into a real incident response workflow?**
```
"CloudTrail alone is just evidence. The production pattern is:
CloudTrail → CloudWatch/EventBridge rule → SNS/PagerDuty/Lambda automation.

Examples:
→ root login detected → page security team immediately
→ security group opened to 0.0.0.0/0 on SSH → revert automatically or quarantine
→ CloudTrail disabled → critical severity incident

The mature answer is that audit logs must be connected to alerting and response,
otherwise you only discover the problem during postmortem."
```

---

blocks.content:
User reports "system is down"
          │
          ▼

          ▼

          ▼
 CloudTrail shows whether a change triggered it
          │
          ▼
 Root cause isolated

sections.title: 🔗 Connects To
sections.rawMarkdown:
```
CloudWatch ← every AWS service: metrics and alarms
CloudWatch Logs ← Lambda/ECS/EC2/API Gateway/apps: operational detail
CloudTrail ← all AWS API calls: control plane audit
X-Ray ← API Gateway/Lambda/ECS/SDK calls: request tracing
Observability → SNS/PagerDuty/Slack: incident alerting
Observability → Auto Scaling/runbooks: automated response

NEXT: Security — KMS, Secrets Manager, WAF, GuardDuty, Shield
The controls that protect your systems once they are in production.
```

---

blocks.content:
CloudWatch ← every AWS service: metrics and alarms
CloudWatch Logs ← Lambda/ECS/EC2/API Gateway/apps: operational detail
CloudTrail ← all AWS API calls: control plane audit
X-Ray ← API Gateway/Lambda/ECS/SDK calls: request tracing
Observability → SNS/PagerDuty/Slack: incident alerting
Observability → Auto Scaling/runbooks: automated response

NEXT: Security — KMS, Secrets Manager, WAF, GuardDuty, Shield
The controls that protect your systems once they are in production.

revisionPoints: CloudWatch ← every AWS service: metrics and alarms
